title: Production Design of Hamnet by Fiona Crombie
text: Fiona Crombie, an Australian production designer with 15 years of film and television experience, created the visual world for director Chloe Zhao's Oscar-nominated film Hamnet. The movie reimagines William Shakespeare's creation of Hamlet following the death of his 11-year-old son in 1596. Crombie explained her expansive role: Production designers are responsible for decisions as varied as the quality of a landscape and the quality of a spoon. She conducted extensive historical research through records, illustrations, paintings, and written descriptions to ensure period accuracy for 16th-century England. The Henley House served as the primary residence, a Tudor-style structure where Crombie described how The house starts as oppressive and opens up with the young family and later contracts and darkens again after Hamnet has died. The set included a separate attic space for the couple's bedroom and Will's writing area. The Globe Theatre reconstruction was 70 percent accurate to the original footprint, featuring painted scenery—a deliberate anachronism Crombie felt was essential to bring the forest and black void to the Globe. Hewlands, Agnes's family home filmed on location in Herefordshire, was created by un-renovated existing architecture while building authentic gardens with medicinal and food plants. Crombie worked with specialized artisans: iron mongers created period hardware, glass blowers produced authentic cloudy glass, and glove-making tools were custom-made. Paul Mescal selected his writing desk and learned to use a quill, while Jessie Buckley learned foraging techniques.
